coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots as fluorescence resonance energy transfer sensors for organophosphate pesticides determination
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xiaoli
Song, Yang
Yan, Xu
Zhu, Chengzhou
Ma, Yongqiang
Du, Dan
Lin, Yuehe
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) obtained from natural organics attract significant attention due to the abundance of carbon sources, varieties of heteroatom doping (such as N, S, P) and good biocompatibility of precursor. In this study, tunable fluorescence emission CQDs originated from chlorophyll were synthesized and characterized. The fluorescence emission can be effectively quenched by gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Thiocholine, which was produced from acetylthiocholine (ATC) by the hydrolysis of butyrylcholinesterase (BChE), could cause the aggregation of Au NPs and the corresponding recovery of FRET-quenched fluorescence emission. The catalytic activity of BChE could be irreversibly inhibited by organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), thus, the recovery effect was reduced. By evaluating the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs, a FRET-based sensing platform for OPs determination was established. Paraoxon was studied as an example of OPs. The sensing platform displayed a linear relationship with the logarithm of the paraoxon concentrations in the range of 0.05–50μgL−1 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.05μgL−1. Real sample study in tap and river water revealed that this sensing platform was repeatable and accurate. The results indicate that the OP sensor is promising for applications in food safety and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Organophosphate pesticide
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of Au NPs and CQDs
FRET quenching of CQDs induced by Au NPs
The recovery effect induced by ATC-BChE-system
OPs determination based on the inhibition effects of BChE
Determination of paraoxon in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image of CQDs and Au NPs. (a) The TEM image of CQDs and insert is the HRTEM image of CQDs. (b) The TEM image of Au NPs.

The characterization of CQDs and Au NPs. (a) The zeta potential of CQDs and Au NPs. (b) The fluorescence lifetime measurement of CQDs in absence and presence of Au NPs. (c) The fluorescence emission spectroscopy of CQDs at different excitation wavelength from 360nm to 460nm. (d) The UV–visible absorbance of Au NPs and fluorescence emission spectroscopy of CQDs excited at 440nm. Inset is the image of CQDs under natural light and 365nm UV light.

FRET of Au NPs on the fluorescence emission of CQDs. (a) The fluorescence emission spectroscopy of CQDs in presence of different concentrations (0nmolL−1 to 2.1nmolL−1) of Au NPs. (b) The quenching percentages (FQ/FQ0) with the different concentrations of Au NPs. Inset: the linear relationship with the logarithmic concentration of Au NPs.

The effect of the concentrations of Au NPs (a), the pH of the solution (b), the incubation temperature (c) and the BChE concentrations (d) on the recovery percentages of FRET-based fluorescence sensing platform.

The fluorescence emission of FRET-based sensing platform in presence of different concentrations (0.05–50μgL−1) of paraoxon (a) and the corresponding inhibition percentage (FI/FI0) of paraoxon (b). Inset: the linear relationship with the logarithmic concentration of paraoxon.
The principle of FRET-based sensing platform for OPs determination.
The determination of paraoxon in tap water and river water.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots as fluorescence resonance energy transfer sensors for organophosphate pesticides determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering, Washington State University, Pullman, WA 99164, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering, Washington State University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, China Agricultural University, Beijing 100193, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Science, China Agricultural University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors are equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) obtained from natural organics attract significant attention due to the abundance of carbon sources, varieties of heteroatom doping (such as N, S, P) and good biocompatibility of precursor. In this study, tunable fluorescence emission CQDs originated from chlorophyll were synthesized and characterized. The fluorescence emission can be effectively quenched by gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) via fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Thiocholine, which was produced from acetylthiocholine (ATC) by the hydrolysis of butyrylcholinesterase (BChE), could cause the aggregation of Au NPs and the corresponding recovery of FRET-quenched fluorescence emission. The catalytic activity of BChE could be irreversibly inhibited by organophosphorus pesticides (OPs), thus, the recovery effect was reduced. By evaluating the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs, a FRET-based sensing platform for OPs determination was established. Paraoxon was studied as an example of OPs. The sensing platform displayed a linear relationship with the logarithm of the paraoxon concentrations in the range of 0.05–50μgL−1 and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.05μgL−1. Real sample study in tap and river water revealed that this sensing platform was repeatable and accurate. The results indicate that the OP sensor is promising for applications in food safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) attract significant attention as potential substrate for biosensing compared to conventional semiconductor QDs due to their attractive advantages including excellent water-solubility, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, simple synthetic routes and tunable fluorescence emission (Baker and Baker, 2010; Lim et al., 2015). The CQDs synthesized from natural organics hold great promise for sensing agent due to the abundance of carbon sources and varieties of heteroatom doping (such as N, S, P). Particularly, citrate (Yang et al., 2011), ascorbic acid (Wu et al., 2012), candle soot (Ray et al., 2009), soy milk (Zhu et al., 2012), orange juice (Sahu et al., 2012), chicken eggs (Wang et al., 2012) and gelatin (Liang et al., 2013) have been used as precursors to synthesize CQDs. These “green” CQDs demonstrate distinguished properties as fluorescence probes in chemical sensings, such as metal ions (Li et al., 2015; Yu et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2015), glucose (He et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2015), glutathione (Gu et al., 2015) and organophosphates pesticides (Hou, et al., 2015a, 2015b; Cai et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2015).

Owing to their inherent advantages mentioned above, CQDs also receive significant interest as fluorescent probes in fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based fluorescent biosensors (Barati et al., 2015). Such FRET-based sensing platform usually contains a fluorophore probe and a nano quencher to form an acceptor-donor FRET pair (Sapsford et al., 2006). When the absorption band of the quencher overlaps with the fluorescence emission band of the donor, the FRET induced fluorescence quenching occurs effectively. Gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) is an ideal nano quencher to establish FRET-based sensing platform owing to their extremely larger extinction coefficient (about 108 cm−1 M−1 or more) and the broad absorption spectrum which overlap with the emission of CQDs (Moores and Goettmann, 2006; Shang and Dong, 2009). Currently, significant efforts have been made to apply such FRET-based sensing platforms for sensitive detection of various analytes such as H2O2 and organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) (Du et al., 2014; Long et al., 2015).

Very recently, Au NPs combined with florescence probes such as rhodamine B, CdTe quantum dots and upconversion nanoparticles have been used for the determination of OPs (Liu et al., 2012; Guo et al., 2014; Long et al., 2015). In this work, CQDs were used for the detection of OPs. Compared with the florescence probes mentioned above, CQDs have the attractive advantages including low toxicity, simple synthetic routes and do not need the excitation light of NIR light. The CQDs were synthesized from commercial chlorophyll and could be effectively quenched by Au NPs via FRET. Based on the FRET between CQDs and Au NPs, a sensitive and convenient sensing platform for OPs determination was developed. The principle of the proposal is illustrated in Scheme 1. Acetylthiocholine (ATC) can be hydrolyzed by BChE to produce thiocholine, which causes the aggregation of Au NPs and the corresponding recovery of the FRET-quenched fluorescence emission. The catalytic activity of BChE is irreversibly inhibited by OPs, resulting in the decrease of Au NPs aggregation and then the reduced recovery of FRET-quenched fluorescence emission. Thus, by evaluating the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs, a FRET-based sensing platform for determination of OPs was established.

Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) hydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), BChE (≥300Umg−1 protein from equine serum), acetylthiocholine iodide (≥98%), iron chloride (FeCl3, ≥98%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, ≥98%), calcium chloride (CaCl2, ≥98%) paraoxon (≥90%), cysteine (≥97%) and chlorophyll (≥90%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Analytical pure potassium chloride (KCl), glucose, sodium chloride (NaCl) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) was obtained from Avantor Performance Materials (USA). Trimethylol aminomethane (≥99.8%) were bought from Fisher Scientific UK Ltd and adjusted with 0.1molL−1 HCl. River water was collected from the local river and tap water was obtained in our laboratory. ELISA 96-well flat-bottom plates were obtained from Fisher Scientific (Pittsburgh, PA). UV–vis measurements and fluorescence emission were obtained on a TECAN XFluor multifunctional microplate reader (Leica Microsystems Inc., IL, USA). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential were performed on a Zeta Sizer Nano ZS. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were measured with a Philips CM200UT transmission electron microscope.

0.2g of commercial chlorophyll was dissolved in 20mL of water. Then the mixture was transferred into PTFE autoclaves and heated at 180°C for 12h. Dark brown solution was obtained after cooled down to room temperature. And then the solution was purified with a dialysis bag (MWCO ~3.0kDa) for 1day. Finally, CQDs were freeze dried to brown solid.

Au NPs were synthesized through the reduction of HAuCl4 by sodium citrate (Grabar et al., 1995). Briefly, trisodium citrate solution (38.8mmolL−1, 5.0mL) was added to boiling HAuCl4 (1mmolL−1, 50mL) solution under continuous magnetic stirring. After the color of the solution changed from yellow to red wine, the mixture was refluxed for 10min before cooled down to room temperature. The molar extinction coefficient (ε) of the Au NPs at 520nm is about 2.7×108 mol−1 cm−1. The final concentration was calculated to be 4.2 nmol L−1 according to Lambert Beer's law (Zhao et al., 2008).

Firstly, BChE solution (25μL, 20ngmL−1) and various concentrations of paraoxon (25μL) were incubated at 37°C (30min). Then ATC (50μL, 1mmolL−1) and tris-HCl buffer (pH=7.5, 10mmolL−1) were added and incubated for another 15min. Finally, CQDs (50μL, 50mgL−1), Au NPs (150μL, 4.2 nmol L−1) and water (150μL) were added. The final concentrations of BChE, ATC, CQDs and Au NPs were 1ngL−1, 0.1mmolL−1, 5mgL−1 and 1.26 nmol L−1, respectively. The fluorescence emission spectrums (excited at 440nm) were recorded. Real sample detection was conducted by investigating the recoveries of different spiking concentrations of paraoxon in tap and river water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Au NPs and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized Au NPs and CQDs were characterized by fluorescence and UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence lifetime, TEM, DLS and zeta potential. Fig. 1 shows the TEM images of CQDs and Au NPs. It indicates that the synthesized CQDs are mostly spherical dots and well dispersed (Fig. 1 a). The HRTEM image (insert of Fig. 1 a) suggests the lattice spacing of CQDs is 0.325nm, which is similar to the distance between the hexagonal planes in graphite crystal structure. It is corresponding to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon, indicating the graphitic nature of CQDs. Fig. 1 b indicates that the synthesized Au NPs are regular, monodisperse, and spherical in shape. DLS measurement confirms that the diameter of AuNPs is about 18nm (Fig. S1). TEM image also shows that Au NPs aggregate (Fig. S2) after incubation with ATC-BChE-system. Sharply visible range absorption bands at around 248 and 317nm are found in the UV–vis spectra of CQDs (Fig. S3), corresponding to absorption of an aromatic π system. FT-IR spectra (Fig. S4) shows that CQDs posse broad absorption bands of O-H and N-H stretching vibrations of amine groups (3425cm−1) and -COOH (1627cm−1), indicating the existence of amino bending and aromatic ring structures on the surface of CQDs. Fig. 2 a shows that the zeta potential of Au NPs is −32.7mV at pH ~7, indicating that Au NPs are negatively charged. The zeta potential of CQDs is +11.6mV, suggesting the CQDs are positively charged. The electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged Au NPs and positively charged CQDs could shorten the distance between Au NPs and CQDs, which formed an efficient FRET donor-acceptor pair and induced effective fluorescence quenching of CQDs. Additionally, fluorescence lifetime measurements (Fig. 2 b) indicate that the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs change in the presence of Au NPs, which suggest that the dynamic quenching occurred between CQDs and Au NPs. It can be seen from Fig. 2 c that the CQDs display excitation wavelength-dependent emission properties, that is, the maximum fluorescence emission shifts from 450 to 520 when the excitation wavelengths change from 360 to 460nm. The maximum fluorescence emission is about 520nm when excited at 440nm. Fig. 2 d shows that the absorption bands of Au NPs (peaking at about 520nm) posse large overlap with the emission of CQDs (excited at 440nm). Therefore, an effective FRET-based fluorescence sensing platform can be established based on the unique tunable emission CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET quenching of CQDs induced by Au NPs
ce:section.ce:para: It is obviously demonstrated from Fig. 3 a that the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs decreases gradually with the increase of Au NPs concentrations. The quenching efficiency was calculated by FQ/FQ0. Where FQ and FQ0 are the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence and absence of Au NPs, respectively. Notably, when the concentration of Au NPs increased to 2.10nmolL−1, the emission intensity of CQDs was quenched nearly 90%. This was attributed to the complementary overlaps of the Au NPs absorption band with the CQDs emission bands, which induced effective FRET quenching of CQDs. Moreover, the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged Au NPs and positively charged CQDs also made a great contribution to the sensing platform. The quenching percentages (FQ/FQ0) display a good linear relationship with the logarithmic concentrations of Au NPs (Fig. 3 b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The recovery effect induced by ATC-BChE-system
ce:section.ce:para: The addition of ATC or BChE has almost no effects on the FRET-based sensing platform (Fig. S5). However, the addition of ATC and BChE together shows a recovery effect on the platform. It is because the BChE-hydrolyzed product (thiocholine) can trigger the aggregation of Au NPs due to the strong Au–SH interaction (Liu et al., 2012; Guo et al., 2014). As a result, Au NPs-induced fluorescence quenching is recovered. The recovery efficiency was calculated by FR/FR0. Where FR0 is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence of Au NPs (2.10nmolL−1) and FR is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence of Au NPs (2.10nmolL−1) and ATC-BChE-system.

A systematic investigation about the recovery effect on the FRET-based sensing platform was performed. The concentrations of Au NPs, the pH of the solution, the incubation temperature and the concentrations of BChE ( Fig. 4) were optimized. Fig. 4 a shows that Au NPs concentrations have obvious influences on the recovery percentages (FR/FR0). The recovery percentages increase first and then decrease with the increase of Au NPs concentrations (0.21–2.1nmolL−1) and reach the highest at 1.26nmolL−1. This is probably because the excess Au NPs which not aggregated cause the fluorescence quench of CQDs. The pH effect on the recovery percentage is shown in Fig. 4 b. It indicates that the recovery percentages increase gradually from pH 6.5 to pH 7.5, but decline dramatically from pH 8.0 to pH 9.5. Incubation temperature is a critical aspect that affects enzyme activity. It can be seen from Fig. 4 c that the recovery percentages reach the highest at 37°C but reduce significantly in low (4°C) and high temperature (50°C). The concentration of BChE is another crucial factor in this FRET-based sensing platform. It demonstrates from Fig. 4 d that the recovery percentages increase gradually with the increase of BChE concentrations (0–2.5ngL−1) and reach a balance at 1.0ngL−1. Therefore, the optimized concentration of Au NPs was 1.26nmolL−1, the pH was 7.5, the temperature was 37°C and the concentration of BChE was 1.0ngL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: OPs determination based on the inhibition effects of BChE
ce:section.ce:para: The inhibition effects of 50μgL−1 of different OPs (isocarbophos, omethoate, phorate, parathion and paraoxon) on the BChE activity were investigated (Fig. S6). The inhibition efficiency was calculated by FI/FI0. Where F I0 is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence of Au NPs-ATC-BChE-system and FI is the fluorescence intensity of CQDs in the presence of OPs. The inhibition percentages (FI/FI0) are between 0.53 and 0.79 (Fig. S6), indicating the OPs have the different level of inhibition effects on BChE. It is noticeable that the inhibition percentages (FI/FI0) of paraoxon is the highest, so paraoxon was studied as an example of OPs. The inhibition percentages (FI/FI0) of paraoxon (10μgL−1) decrease with the time increasing and reach a balance within 30min (Fig. S7 ), indicating that BChE activity is gradually restrained by paraoxon. Fig. 5 a displays the fluorescent intensity in the presence of 1mmolL−1 ATC with variable concentrations of paraoxon. It shows that when the concentrations of paraoxon increase, the fluorescent emission intensity decreases gradually (Fig. 5 b). The inset in Fig. 5 b shows that the inhibition percentages are in linear correlation with the logarithm of the paraoxon concentrations (0.05–50μgL−1). The coefficient of determination (R2) is 0.994 and the LOD is 0.05μgL−1 (0.18nmolL−1). The LOD was obtained by the equation LOD=(3σ/s), where σ is the standard deviation of blank signals (n=9) and s is the slope of the calibration curve (Yan et al., 2014). Compared with other methods such as electrochemical, fluorescence sensor, liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometric (LC-MS-MS) and ELISA, the performance of the proposed method is comparable or even better (Table S1).

The anti-interference capability of the method was investigated to evaluate the specificity of this platform (Fig. S8). Paraoxon (10μgL−1) and some common existing substances in the environmental water samples such as cysteine, KCl, glucose, NaCl, FeCl3, MgCl2 and CaCl2 (0.5mmolL−1 in the system) are added to test the anti-interference capability. The results indicated that these common existing substances have no significant effects on this FRET-based sensing platform at the spiked level (0.5mmolL−1 in the system).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of paraoxon in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations (0.25–5μgL−1) of paraoxon spiked in the local river and tap river water samples were investigated and the results are shown in Table 1. The recoveries range from 90.0% to 103.2% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) less than 3%. It reveals that this method is an accurate, repeatable and simple method for paraoxon determination.

In this work, CQDs were synthesized from chlorophyll and were used for the determination of OPs based on FRET. By adjusting the excitation wavelength, maximum fluorescence emission of CQDs was tuned to 520nm. The fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs decreased significantly in the presence of Au NPs due to the complementary overlaps of the emission bands of CQDs with absorption bands of Au NPs (peaking at 520nm) and the electrostatic interaction between negatively charged Au NPs and positively charged CQDs. Upon addition of ATC and BChE, the FRET-quenched fluorescence was recovered. It was due to the thiocholine generated by enzymatic catalysis reaction caused the aggregation of Au NPs. By evaluating the inhibition effect on the activity of BChE and the fluorescence emission intensity, a simple and sensitive method for paraoxon determination was established. This study developed a facile FRET-sensing platform for OPs and other chemical detection due to the tunable emission fluorescence CQDs. It is expected that OP sensor developed from this work will have a great potential for applications in food safety and environmental monitoring.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention/National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (CDC/NIOSH) Grant R21OH010768. We would like to acknowledge the China Scholarship Council for providing scholarship to work at WSU.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.03.010.
